1: The word-of-the-LORD formula introduces the first Tyre oracle of this chapter, addressed to the prince (nasi) of Tyre. The distinction between prince (nasi, Ezekiel 28:1-10) and king (melek, Ezekiel 28:11-19) is significant: the prince-oracle addresses the historical ruler (probably Ethbaal III, who ruled during Nebuchadnezzar's siege); the king-oracle employs a more elevated register that exceeds the historical ruler. Christ is both the antitype of the pride condemned in Ezekiel 28:1-10 and the fulfillment of the perfection described and lost in Ezekiel 28:12-19.
2: “Because your heart is proud and you have said, 'I am a god'” — the primal sin of hubris: claiming divine status. This is the sin of Adam (Genesis 3:5: “you will be like God”), of the builders of Babel (Genesis 11:4), of Pharaoh (Exodus 5:2: “Who is the LORD that I should obey him?”), and will be the sin of the man of lawlessness (2 Thessalonians 2:4: “who opposes and exalts himself against every so-called god or object of worship, so that he takes his seat in the temple of God, proclaiming himself to be God”). Christ, who truly is God, “did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped” (Philippians 2:6) — the exact reversal of the Tyre-prince's sin: the human who claimed to be God vs. the God who became human.
3: “Behold, you are wiser than Daniel — no secret is hidden from you” — the ironic comparison to Daniel, the living standard of wisdom in Ezekiel's time (also in Ezekiel 14:14, 20). The prince of Tyre claims a wisdom that surpasses even the wisest of humans. Paul's response to this claim, applied to Christ: “in whom are hidden all the treasures of wisdom and knowledge” (Colossians 2:3). What the prince of Tyre claimed falsely — wisdom that surpasses Daniel, all secrets known — Christ possesses truly. The one who knew the secrets of every person's heart (John 2:24-25: “he knew all people ... he himself knew what was in man”) is the true answer to the wisdom-claim.
4: “By your wisdom and understanding you have gained wealth” — Tyre's wisdom produced commercial prosperity, paralleling Solomon's wisdom that produced material wealth (1 Kings 3:12-13: “I give you a wise and discerning heart ... I also give you what you have not asked, both riches and honor”). But Tyre's wisdom is self-attributing: it is credited to the prince's own intelligence, not to YHWH's gift. Proverbs 8 personifies Wisdom as the one through whom kings reign and wealth is found (Proverbs 8:15-21); Christ is “the wisdom of God” (1 Corinthians 1:24) in whom this figure is embodied. True wisdom produces true wealth (Proverbs 8:18) — Christ's riches are “unsearchable” (Ephesians 3:8).
5: “Your heart has become proud because of your wealth” — the sequence wisdom → wealth → pride is the diagnostic pattern. Deuteronomy 8:17-18 warns Israel against exactly this: “beware lest you say in your heart, 'My power and the might of my hand have gotten me this wealth.' You shall remember the LORD your God, for it is he who gives you power to get wealth.” Christ addresses the same pride in the parable of the rich fool (Luke 12:16-21) and warns: “a person's life does not consist in the abundance of his possessions” (Luke 12:15). The wealth that becomes pride is the seed of the judgment that follows in Ezekiel 28:6-10.
6: “Because you have set your heart as the heart of a god” — the therefore-of-judgment follows the because-of-sin. The structural logic of covenant lawsuit: accusation (Ezekiel 28:2-5) → verdict (Ezekiel 28:6) → sentence (Ezekiel 28:7-10). Christ absorbs this lawsuit structure in his own passion: he is accused (John 19:7), sentenced (John 19:15-16), and executed — but the sentence he receives is not for his own pride but for ours. The covenant-lawsuit that the proud deserve falls on the innocent one, reversing the expected outcome.
7: “I will bring strangers against you, the most ruthless of the nations; they will draw their swords against the beauty of your wisdom and defile your splendor” — Babylon against Tyre's wisdom. What looks like military invasion is the divine deconstruction of false wisdom. Paul uses similar language for the cross: “God chose what is foolish in the world to shame the wise; God chose what is weak in the world to shame the strong” (1 Corinthians 1:27). The “ruthless nations” that YHWH brings against proud wisdom are a type of the divine folly of the cross that defeats human wisdom more effectively than any army.
8: “They will thrust you down into the Pit, and you will die the death of the slain in the heart of the seas” — the Pit (shachat) is Sheol, the place of the dead. The prince who claimed to sit in the seat of gods is thrust into the lowest place. This is the precise reversal of the Magnificat pattern: “he has brought down the mighty from their thrones and exalted those of humble estate” (Luke 1:52). Christ himself enters the Pit — “you will not abandon my soul to Hades” (Acts 2:27, Psalms 16:10) — but rises from it, unlike the prince of Tyre whose descent is permanent.
9: “Will you still say, 'I am a god,' before the one who kills you? But you will be a mere man in the hand of the one who wounds you” — the death-chamber revelation: divinity claims dissolve before the sword. This is the prophetic demythologization of human pretension: in the moment of death, all claims to divine status are exposed as lies. Christ's death on the cross appeared to be the same demythologization of his divine claims (Mark 15:29-32: “let him come down from the cross and we will believe him”). But the resurrection is YHWH's counter-verdict: what looked like the exposure of Christ's messianic claim as false was the vindication of that claim in the most definitive possible way.
10: “You will die the death of the uncircumcised by the hand of foreigners” — the maximum shame of the Ezekielian Sheol-gallery (see Ezekiel 32). The prince who claimed divine status will die in the company of the uncircumcised — those without covenant identity. This is the judgment that falls on all who claim divine status without possessing it. Christ, who is truly divine, willingly accepted the shameful death of a criminal (crucifixion was the death of slaves and rebels, not of citizens and the covenanted) — absorbing the shame that should fall on those who falsely claim divine status, so that those who genuinely belong to him are covered by his righteousness.
11: The second word-of-the-LORD formula introduces the king-oracle (Ezekiel 28:11-19) with a higher register than the prince-oracle. The shift from “prince” to “king” signals a different level of discourse: the king-oracle will exceed what can be said of any merely human ruler. The dual oracle structure (prince → king) mirrors other prophetic dual-level oracles (Isaiah 14:4-21 speaks both of the king of Babylon and, many interpreters argue, of a transcendent figure behind him; Daniel 10:13, 20-21 speaks of the spiritual “prince” of Persia and Greece behind the earthly kings).
12: “You were the seal of perfection, full of wisdom and perfect in beauty” — these attributes cannot apply to any historical king of Tyre: no human ruler has ever been “the seal of perfection” (totem tachlit). The figure described is one who began in a state of genuine, complete perfection — wisdom without limit, beauty without flaw. The only being to whom this can be said truthfully is Christ: “the radiance of the glory of God and the exact imprint of his nature” (Hebrews 1:3); “the image of the invisible God” (Colossians 1:15). What the king of Tyre's ode describes as a state now lost, Christ embodies as a permanent identity.
13: “You were in Eden, the garden of God” — the placement of this figure in Eden, the primordial divine garden, exceeds any historical king. The gemstones listed (ruby, topaz, crystal, beryl, onyx, jasper, lapis lazuli, turquoise, emerald) are nearly identical to those on the high priest's breastplate (Exodus 28:17-20), suggesting this figure was adorned as a priestly guardian of the divine presence. “The craftsmanship of your settings and engravings was prepared in you on the day you were created” — a created being, but created for the highest possible role. Many interpreters (from Origen to modern scholars) read this as describing the being behind the king of Tyre: a guardian cherub who had access to Eden but fell. Christologically, Christ is the one who restores Eden-access (Revelation 2:7: “to the one who conquers I will grant to eat of the tree of life, which is in the paradise of God”).
14: “You were the anointed guardian cherub; I had appointed you. You were on the holy mountain of God; you walked among the stones of fire” — the cherub (kerub) was the specific category of heavenly being that guarded sacred space: the cherubim on the ark (Exodus 25:18-22), the cherubim in the Holy of Holies (1 Kings 6:23-28), the cherubim that drove Adam and Eve from Eden (Genesis 3:24). “Anointed” (mimshach) — the same root as Messiah (mashiach). The anointed guardian cherub was set apart for the highest function: protecting the divine presence. Christ is the anointed one (Psalms 2:2; Acts 4:26-27) who also guards and gives access to the divine presence — not by excluding sinners from the throne but by opening the way for them through his blood (Hebrews 10:19-22).
15: “You were blameless in your ways from the day you were created until iniquity was found in you” — the blamelessness that characterized this being from creation is the perfection Christ alone permanently retains. Jesus was “without sin” (Hebrews 4:15), “holy, innocent, unstained, separated from sinners” (Hebrews 7:26), “a lamb without blemish or spot” (1 Peter 1:19). The “until iniquity was found” applies to the figure in the oracle, not to Christ — but the contrast is precisely the Christological point: where the guardian cherub fell from blamelessness, Christ maintained it through the full course of his life, qualifying him to be the sufficient sacrifice.
16: “Through the abundance of your trade you were filled inwardly with violence, and you sinned; so I cast you out as profane from the mountain of God” — the fall: inward violence, sin, expulsion from the holy mountain. Luke 10:18: “I saw Satan fall like lightning from heaven” — Jesus's reference to a primordial expulsion from the divine mountain is the NT's closest parallel to this verse. The casting out of the guardian cherub from the mountain of God is what Christ came to undo: he entered the territory of the fallen guardian (the world, which John describes as lying in the power of the evil one, 1 John 5:19) and defeated him (John 12:31; Revelation 20:10).
17: “Your heart was proud because of your beauty; you corrupted your wisdom for the sake of your splendor. I have cast you to the ground and exposed you before kings” — the pride of beauty corrupting wisdom is the original pattern of spiritual fall: the created glory becoming the occasion for self-exaltation rather than YHWH-glorification. Christ is the one who “came to serve and not to be served” (Mark 10:45) and whose beauty was hidden in humiliation (Isaiah 53:2: “he had no form or majesty that we should look at him, and no beauty that we should desire him”). The one whose eternal beauty was uncorrupted by pride was exposed before kings — not in shame, but in the definitive act of divine love.
18: “By the abundance of your iniquities ... you defiled your sanctuaries; therefore I brought out fire from your midst — it consumed you, and I turned you to ashes on the earth” — fire from within consuming the sinful figure. This is the self-consuming nature of sin: the fire of judgment begins from the inside, not the outside. Christ absorbed this fire at Golgotha — “our God is a consuming fire” (Hebrews 12:29) and Christ bore the divine fire on behalf of sinners, so that those who are united to him are not consumed. The ashes on the earth where Tyre's glory stood become the ashes of repentance from which resurrection rises.
19: “All who knew you among the peoples are appalled at you; you have become a horror and shall be no more forever” — the permanent end of the one who fell from perfection. This permanent end is the theological statement about the irreversibility of the fall from the guardian cherub's original estate — unlike human sinners for whom restoration is possible (and for whom Christ came), the figure behind the king of Tyre oracle has passed the point of no return. Revelation 20:10 confirms this: the devil “will be tormented day and night forever and ever.” Christ's redemption is for the human beings who were deceived by the fallen cherub, not for the cherub itself — this is the scope and limit of the atonement's application.
20: The third word-of-the-LORD formula introduces the brief Sidon oracle (Ezekiel 28:21-23). Sidon, Tyre's northern neighbor, is addressed in a shorter oracle without the extended lament of the Tyre oracles. The structure of Ezekiel 25-28 systematically covers the nations surrounding Israel: Ammon, Moab, Edom, Philistia (Ezekiel 25), Tyre (Ezekiel 26-28), and Sidon (Ezekiel 28:20-23). Christ's gospel eventually reaches all these nations (Romans 15:16-19: “from Jerusalem and all the way around to Illyricum I have fulfilled the ministry of the gospel of Christ”; Acts 27:3 mentions Sidon as one of Paul's ports of call).
21: “Son of man, set your face against Sidon and prophesy against it” — the confrontation formula applied to Sidon. Sidon was the northern Phoenician city-state, older than Tyre and historically important in the patriarchal period (Genesis 10:15: Sidon is Canaan's firstborn son). Jesus himself traveled to the region of Tyre and Sidon and healed a Canaanite/Syrophoenician woman (Matthew 15:21-28; Mark 7:24-30) — the judgment-oracle over Sidon becomes the location of a healing that Jesus uses to expand his mission beyond Israel. The judgments of the OT become the opportunities of the NT.
22: “I will be glorified in your midst ... I will display my holiness in her” — YHWH's holiness is displayed through judgment: “I am holy in those who are near me, and before all the people I will be glorified” (Leviticus 10:3, after Nadab and Abihu's death). Christ is “the Holy One of God” (Mark 1:24; John 6:69) whose holiness is displayed not through punitive destruction but through the cross, where divine holiness and divine love meet in perfect unity. Sidon witnesses YHWH's glory; the whole world witnesses Christ's glory in the cross and resurrection (John 12:23-24: “the hour has come for the Son of Man to be glorified ... unless a grain of wheat falls into the earth and dies”).
23: “The slain will fall in her midst by the sword that comes against her from every side — and they will know that I am the LORD” — the knowledge formula after judgment. The historical fulfillment came through Babylonian, Persian, and Seleucid conquests of Sidon (Sidon was razed by Artaxerxes III in 345 BCE). The “they will know” formula finds its deepest fulfillment in the crucifixion narrative: the Roman centurion who witnessed the manner of Christ's death said “truly this man was the Son of God” (Mark 15:39) — the knowledge of YHWH's identity coming through the death of the one who bears his name.
24: “There will no longer be a painful brier or a piercing thorn from any of the surrounding peoples who showed contempt for Israel” — the removal of briers and thorns. The thorn-brier imagery goes back to the curse of Genesis 3:18: “thorns and thistles it shall bring forth for you.” The surrounding nations are described as thorns in Israel's side (Numbers 33:55; Joshua 23:13). Christ wore a crown of thorns at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:29; John 19:2) — visually absorbing the curse of the thorns, taking onto himself the “brier” that the nations represent, so that “there will no longer be a painful brier.” The removal of the thorn-nations in Ezekiel's oracle is accomplished Christologically through Christ's absorption of the curse.
25: “When I gather the house of Israel from the peoples among whom they have been scattered, and display my holiness in them before the eyes of the nations, then they will dwell in their own land that I gave to my servant Jacob” — the restoration promise after the judgment oracles. The gathering of scattered Israel is the eschatological gathering that the NT applies to Christ: “he died ... to gather into one the children of God who are scattered abroad” (John 11:52). The Jacob-land promise is fulfilled in the new creation where “the meek shall inherit the earth” (Matthew 5:5; cf. Psalms 37:11) — the land given to Jacob's offspring becomes, in Christ, the renewed earth given to all who are Abraham's children by faith.
26: “They will dwell in it safely and build houses and plant vineyards. They will live with confidence when I have executed judgments against all their surrounding neighbors who showed them contempt. And they will know that I am the LORD their God” — the concluding restoration promise. Safety, houses, vineyards, confidence — the covenant blessing formula from Leviticus 26:5 (“you shall live in your land securely”) and Micah 4:4 (“every man shall sit under his vine and under his fig tree”). Christ is the “prince of peace” (Isaiah 9:6) through whom this security is established — not by military elimination of the nations but by their conversion through the gospel (Revelation 21:24: “by its light will the nations walk”). The knowledge formula that closes the oracle — “they will know that I am the LORD their God” — is the full covenant-restoration formula: not merely knowledge of YHWH's power but relational knowledge of YHWH as their God, achieved through Christ's reconciling work.
